main_ingredients: kadhai masala (coriander seeds, dried red chilli, cumin)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Kadhai Veg is approximately 300 g, comprising a generous bowl of spiced mixed vegetables served with bread or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Mixed Vegetable Curry / Vegetable Sabzi
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Kadhai Veg is a North Indian wok-cooked vegetable preparation. No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for 'Kadhai Veg' by name. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 2017 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' data for mixed vegetable curry (cooked, oil-based, spiced), which is the closest defensible analogue. The dish typically contains capsicum, onion, tomato, and spices cooked in oil; values reflect a moderate-oil preparation. Energy and macros are consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference for cooked mixed vegetable curry (FDC ID 2103689) used as cross-check.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — mixed vegetable dishes and analogous low-GI vegetable preparations
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kadhai Veg. GI estimated at 45 based on analogous mixed vegetable curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Mixed vegetable dishes with moderate oil and spice content and low starch load consistently fall in the GI 40–55 range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 28.5) / 100 ≈ 12.8, using 300 g serving × 9.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 28.5 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~960 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 300 g restaurant portion of Kadhai Veg may contribute approximately 960 mg sodium, representing roughly 40–48% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2400 mg; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) and moderate carbohydrate content (~9.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kadhai Veg has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 45 and a moderate glycemic load of ~12.8 per restaurant serving, making it a suitable vegetable dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed with a low-GI staple and without added sugar-based sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.8 g per 100 g), cholesterol-free, high vegetable content with antioxidant spices
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kadhai Veg is low in saturated fat and contains no cholesterol, with a predominantly unsaturated fat profile from cooking oil; the high vegetable content and antioxidant-rich spices (capsicum, tomato, coriander) support a heart-healthy dietary pattern, though sodium intake should be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kadhai Veg (also spelled Kadai Veg or Karahi Veg) is a popular North Indian vegetable dish cooked in a kadhai, a traditional thick-bottomed Indian wok with steep sides. The dish features a colourful medley of vegetables — most commonly capsicum (bell pepper), onion, tomato, and sometimes paneer or seasonal vegetables — stir-fried and simmered in a bold, aromatic spice base known as kadhai masala, which is freshly ground from whole coriander seeds, dried red chillies, and cumin. Ginger, garlic, garam masala, and fresh coriander leaves are added to build depth of flavour. The cooking technique involves high-heat sautéing in oil, which imparts a characteristic smoky, slightly charred edge to the vegetables while preserving their texture. The resulting dish is semi-dry to semi-gravy in consistency, vibrant in colour, and intensely spiced. Nutritionally, Kadhai Veg is a moderate-energy dish (~98 kcal per 100 g) with a low glycemic index (~45), making it suitable for blood glucose management. It provides dietary fibre (~2.8 g per 100 g), vitamin C (~28 mg), and beta-carotene from capsicum and tomato. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations (~320 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal. It is typically served with Indian flatbreads (roti, naan, paratha) or steamed basmati rice as part of a main meal. In Singapore, Kadhai Veg is served at Indian restaurants and food delivery platforms as a main course vegetable option.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Kadhai Veg?
Is Kadhai Veg good for diabetics?
What vegetables are in Kadhai Veg?
What spices are used in Kadhai Veg?

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists specifically for Kadhai Veg by name. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry data and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central cooked mixed vegetable curry entries. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable mixed vegetable dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the dish composition is well-defined and the analogous sources are closely matched.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 2017 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' mixed vegetable curry data; no exact Kadhai Veg entry exists in any approved primary source. 2. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed vegetable curry dishes; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients in the standard preparation; confirmed zero for vegetable-only preparation. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as plant-based vegetable dishes contain negligible vitamin D; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN vegetable dish data. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as this dish uses standard cooking oil without hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per source. 7. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a vegetable-only dish with no animal products; naturally zero. 8. The cuisine_tag 'Chicken Dish' in the input record appears to be a data error from the field survey seed record; Kadhai Veg is a vegetarian dish and this tag has been disregarded. 9. Serving size of 300 g is estimated based on typical Indian restaurant main course portions; no standardised serving size is defined in the source. 10. Glycemic load per serving = (45 × 28.5) / 100 = 12.8, where 28.5 g is carbohydrate per 300 g serving.
